Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic, even though I put in a lot of hours each week. I'd usually block out time in the evenings after work and on weekends, sitting down with my printed notes and readings. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd copy out key concepts and rewrite my notes multiple times. The routine was there in terms of hours, but I couldn't always make it to actual classes because of my schedule and other responsibilities.
Q2: It varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying the same times every day, but then something would come up—work shifts, family stuff—and I'd have to cram everything into a few days. Deadlines definitely pushed me to study more intensely, even if my overall pattern wasn't perfectly regular. I tried to stay steady, but life doesn't always cooperate when you have limited resources.
Q3: I'd usually log in when I needed to check announcements or see if any new materials were posted. Sometimes I'd go in to look at discussion prompts or see what other students were saying. If I knew an assignment was coming up, I'd log in to download the instructions and any readings I needed. It wasn't always about actively doing something—sometimes just checking in to make sure I wasn't missing anything important.
Q4: There were stretches where logging in became almost automatic, like part of my morning routine with coffee. Once I got into a rhythm with the discussion posts, I'd check in regularly to see responses and add my thoughts. But honestly, that habit would break whenever my anxiety spiked or I got overwhelmed with other things. It never fully became second nature for me the way I wished it would.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me because I learn best by reading and writing things down. I appreciated when instructors posted detailed notes or transcripts alongside videos. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could process ideas by writing out my thoughts and seeing how others interpreted the same material. Videos were okay, but I preferred having text I could highlight and annotate.
Q6: I often skipped or minimized my use of video content because it takes so long to watch and I can't easily search through it later. Some of the optional supplementary materials I'd ignore if I was already feeling overwhelmed—I just didn't have the mental bandwidth. I also didn't engage much with peer interaction features beyond what was required because my anxiety made me hesitant to reach out too much.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a clear connection to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. When something felt practical and applicable, I was more motivated. If it seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades, I'd deprioritize it—especially when I was already stretched thin with everything else going on.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're anxious about keeping up and have limited time and resources, you focus on what's required first. Required activities had a sense of urgency that optional ones didn't. I'd sometimes feel guilty for not doing the optional stuff, but I had to be realistic about what I could handle.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, print them out when I could afford to, and work from my handwritten notes constantly. I used notebooks filled with rewritten concepts and summaries. The LMS was more like a portal to get materials rather than where I actually did my learning—my real study life happened at my desk with paper and pen.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes or reworking practice problems on paper, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd read through downloaded chapters multiple times, annotating and summarizing, but the LMS would just show I downloaded the file once. My most productive study sessions were completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd access a reading or resource once to download it, then I'd work with it offline for days or even weeks. I preferred having physical copies or saved files I could access without internet, especially since my resources are limited and I can't always rely on stable connectivity. One download could represent hours of subsequent study.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety got really bad and engaging with anything felt impossible. When I was worried about money or dealing with personal stress, I'd avoid the course because it felt like one more thing demanding something from me. My attendance dropped during those times, and I'd fall behind, which only made the anxiety worse. It became a cycle that was hard to break.
Q13: All of those things affected me at different points. Fatigue was a big one—I'd be exhausted from work and other responsibilities, and the idea of logging in felt overwhelming. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS rather than confront what I didn't understand. Competing responsibilities meant the course often got pushed down my priority list even when I knew I shouldn't let that happen.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or watch part of a video but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be logged in but thinking about bills or other worries, not actually absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd open readings and stare at them without really processing the words. Being physically present in the LMS didn't mean I was mentally engaged.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. My anxiety made starting things really hard—I'd worry about doing them wrong or not being good enough. The low assignment completion rate reflects this struggle. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own problems. It wasn't laziness; it was being paralyzed by worry.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my avoidance patterns. When something was due soon, the fear of failing would finally outweigh the anxiety about doing the work. I'd have intense bursts of LMS activity right before deadlines, downloading everything I needed and trying to catch up. It wasn't healthy, but deadlines were the external pressure I needed to actually submit things.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would boost my mood and make me want to engage more, but critical feedback could send me into an anxiety spiral. After the CBT sessions I've been doing, I've gotten a bit better at taking feedback constructively, but it's still hard. Sometimes I'd avoid checking grades because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work around my schedule, but bad because without strict structure, my anxiety and low motivation made it easy to fall behind. I needed more scaffolding and check-ins than the course provided. The flexibility assumed a level of self-direction that I struggled to maintain consistently.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement easier because I could study in my own way offline. But the lack of structured reminders or check-ins made it harder—I'd lose track of where I was supposed to be. Discussion requirements helped because they forced me to engage regularly. More frequent low-stakes deadlines would have helped me stay on track better.
Q20: I can definitely relate to this pattern. Sometimes you're logging in constantly because you're anxious and checking things obsessively, but you're too overwhelmed to actually complete and submit work. Or you might be reading and preparing but struggling to pull everything together into a finished assignment. High activity can reflect anxiety and uncertainty as much as productive engagement.
Q21: This makes sense to me because so much learning happens outside the LMS. Someone might download materials once and study extensively offline, or they might be naturally quick at understanding concepts and not need to spend as much time on the platform. I think my exam score was decent partly because of all my offline studying, even though my LMS activity and assignment completion didn't reflect that effort.
Q22: A course log can show patterns of access—when you logged in, what you clicked on, how long you spent. But it completely misses the quality of engagement or what's happening in your head. It can't see that I spent three hours with a printed PDF or that I was too anxious to concentrate even when I was logged in. It captures actions but not understanding or emotional state.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, when I want to understand something deeply, when I'm thinking about course concepts even outside of study time. For me, engagement is as much emotional and mental as it is behavioral—it's feeling invested in my own learning, even when it's hard.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affects everything I do in this course, or how the CBT sessions have been helping me cope. It wouldn't capture the hours I spend with my handwritten notes, or the mental energy it takes me to even open my laptop some days. My engagement is real but it's largely invisible—it happens in my head and on paper, not on a screen.
